cves.evidence_snippet: CVE2015- 2051 EUVD2015- 2164 10 (v2.0) D-Link D-Link DIR645 Router

ttps.evidence_snippet: Phishing including vishing, malspam, and malvertising accounting for about 60% of observed cases
Exploitation of vulnerabilities 21.3% remains a prevalent intrusion vector; widespread campaigns rapidly weaponising them within days of their disclosure
Cybercriminals increasingly targeted third-party providers, such as Digital Services, as an opportunity to optimise the efficiency of their attacks
ClickFix-style scams tricked users into executing PowerShell commands under the pretext of human verification, leading to the installation of information stealers and loaders
Info-stealers sold on cybercriminal marketplaces remained a consistent threat vector, primarily facilitating credential theft, session hijacking and access brokering
Attackers actively leveraged malicious browser extensions and npm packages, scanning public repositories to compromise developers environments
Ransomware incidents resulted in prolonged disruptions to business continuity including media, manufacturing, and health sectors
DDoS attacks make up about 76.7% of recorded cases; category overwhelmingly driven by hacktivist groups
Mobile spyware like EagleMsgSpy used by Chinese Public Security Bureaus to aggregate identity, communications, and location data

summary: ENISA Threat Landscape 2025 (TLPCLEAR, October 2025) covers July 2024–June 2025, analysing 4,875 curated incidents affecting EU MSs. Social engineering dominates initial access: phishing (vishing, malspam, malvertising) accounts for ~60% of cases, while vulnerability exploitation accounts for 21.3% and is far more likely to lead to malware deployment (68% of vulnerability cases vs 23% of phishing). DDoS (mainly hacktivist-led) represents 76.7% of all incidents; intrusions 17.8%, dominated by cybercrime. Mobile threats are the largest category (42.4% of threat types), followed by web (27.3%), OT (18.2%) and supply chain (10.6%). Cybercrime (13.4% of incidents) is led by ransomware (81.1% of cybercrime, 82 families) with Akira, SafePay, and Qilin most deployed; data breaches (15.2%) largely follow ransomware. LockBit3 dominance ended after compromise and leaks; LockBit4 emerged; 8Base, BlackBasta, Hunters International, RansomHub all disrupted or fragmented. Info-stealers (RedLine, META, Lumma) underpin access brokering, with Operation Magnus and later Lumma takedown disrupting but not stopping the market. State-aligned activity: Russia-, China-, DPRK-, and Iran-nexus intrusion sets intensified espionage against EU telecoms, logistics, manufacturing, finance; China-nexus groups exploited Ivanti EPMM CVE-2025-4428 and Cisco 0-days; Lazarus abused npm supply chains.

timeline.event: Start of reporting period for ENISA Threat Landscape 2025
timeline.evidence_snippet: The ETL 2025 provides an overview of the European cyber threat ecosystem from July 2024 to June 2025

timeline.event: Operation Magnus (RedLine/META takedown) disrupts infostealer market
timeline.evidence_snippet: Operation Magnus in October 2024... led to the dismantling and seizure of the infrastructure of RedLine and META

timeline.event: Plus Service breach impacts Italian transport ticketing (MoM, Busitalia Veneto, ATM Milano)
timeline.evidence_snippet: In March 2025, Plus Service, an external provider... suffered a data breach... notably resulted in the Mobilita di Marca MoM ticketing systems being paralysed for two days

timeline.event: LockBit programme compromised; LockBit3 activity ceases, LockBit4 appears
timeline.evidence_snippet: In May 2025, the LockBit ransomware programme was reportedly compromised resulting in the leak of their internal database... absence of claims... since 27 May 2025 and the emergence of LockBit4 since April

timeline.event: Lumma infrastructure takedown operation coordinated by Europol and Microsoft
timeline.evidence_snippet: In May 2025, joint international LEA action coordinated by Europol led to the seizing, takedown, suspension and blocking of approximately 2 300 malicious domains in Lummas infrastructure

timeline.event: End of ENISA TL 2025 reporting period / cut-off for incidents
timeline.evidence_snippet: The reporting period referred to spans from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025, with the cut-off date being 30 June 2025
